chunks.text: The world population has passed eight billion.
Yet more than 800 million people still lack enough food and suffer from hunger.
Agriculture is one of the oldest industries of humanity and a foundation of civilization.
Since the invention of irrigation farming in ancient Mesopotamia
humanity has multiplied crop yields many times over through improved varieties and new technology.
In the " Green Revolution " of the twentieth century
high - yield varieties and chemical fertilizer dramatically increased food production in Asia and Africa.
But at the same time
the excessive use of pesticide and fertilizer has contaminated soil and water
causing long - term problems.
In recent years, both organic farming and genetically modified gene technology have drawn attention.
The former is kinder to the environment but difficult for mass production
the latter raises yields but leaves concerns about safety.
Another major problem is food waste.
In developed countries, about one third of all food produced is thrown away.
In a world where people go hungry, this is a serious contradiction.
Solving the food problem requires more than increasing production.
Improvement in distribution, reduction of waste
and a change in the awareness of each consumer are all needed.
To feed the earth in a sustainable way is the greatest challenge of the coming century.
